title: Change in CO₂ emissions and GDP
subtitle: [Consumption-based emissions](#dod:consumptionbasedemissions) are national emissions that have been adjusted for trade. This measures [fossil fuel and industry emissions](#dod:fossilemissions). [Land-use change emissions](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) are not included.
note: GDP is measured in [international-$](#dod:int_dollar_abbreviation) at 2021 prices to account for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Change in CO₂ emissions and GDP

Consumption-based emissions are national emissions that have been adjusted for trade. This measures fossil fuel and industry emissions. Land-use change emissions are not included.

This multi-panel line chart tracks the percentage change in GDP, CO₂ emissions, and consumption-based CO₂ emissions from 1990 to 2024 for Sweden, the United Kingdom, the United States, France, Germany, and the European Union (27). The vertical axis ranges from -50% to +150%, with a horizontal line marking the 0% baseline. In every panel, the teal line representing GDP shows a consistent upward trajectory, ending between approximately +50% and +125% by 2024. Conversely, the blue line for CO₂ emissions and the red line for consumption-based CO₂ emissions generally trend downward or remain flat relative to the 1990 baseline. For the United States, GDP rises to over +100%, while both emission lines hover near the 0% mark, dipping slightly below by the end of the period. In the European Union (27), Germany, France, and the United Kingdom, GDP increases range from roughly +40% to +60%, while both emission metrics fall into negative territory, reaching between -20% and -40%. Sweden displays a similar pattern with GDP growth exceeding +80% and emissions dropping below -20%. The red and blue lines often track closely together across all regions, frequently overlapping or running parallel in the lower half of the charts. GDP is measured in international-$ at 2021 prices to account for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. The visual data illustrates a divergence where economic output grows while emission levels decrease or stabilize across the selected economies.
